title: Dominion
resolution_core: Action-Buy-Cleanup deckbuilding turns; buy from fixed kingdom supply piles

vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.fixed_supply_piles: Ten kingdom card piles plus fixed Treasure (Copper/Silver/Gold) and Victory (Estate/Duchy/Province) piles are laid out at setup; every purchasable card is visible for the whole game and piles can empty.
vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.starter_deck_upgrades: Every player begins with an identical 10-card deck of 7 Coppers and 3 Estates and improves it by buying kingdom cards that get shuffled in on later reshuffles.
vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.deck_thinning: Trashing is a dedicated rules concept: cards like Chapel (trash up to 4 cards) and Remodel remove weak Coppers and Estates to concentrate the deck's quality.
vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.deck_cycling_tempo: When your draw pile empties you shuffle your discard pile into a new deck, so players time purchases so key cards return in the very next shuffle.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: Turns are played from a five-card hand; sequencing matters because playing Village (+1 Card, +2 Actions) before Smithy (+3 Cards) keeps action chains alive.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_replenishment: Cleanup always refills the hand to exactly five cards from the draw pile, regardless of how many cards were played or discarded that turn.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.discard_costs: Several cards charge discards as an activation cost, such as Cellar (discard any number of cards, draw that many) — and Militia attacks force rivals down to three cards.
vector_explanations.economy.card.deck_building: Dominion is the genre's origin point: cards bought during the Buy phase go to your discard pile and enter your circulating deck at the next shuffle.
vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.combo_scaling: Individual kingdom cards are weak alone but multiply together, e.g. +Action villages enabling several terminal draw cards to fire in a single turn.
vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.engine_to_points: Victory cards are dead draws while the game runs, so the defining skill is timing the 'greening' pivot from engine parts to Provinces and Duchies.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.cleanup_reset: The C of the ABC turn: in Cleanup you discard your hand and every card played this turn, then draw a fresh hand of five to end your turn.
vector_explanations.actions.points.variable_action_counts: You start each turn with exactly 1 Action and 1 Buy, but +1 Action / +1 Buy effects (Village, Market, Festival) expand those allowances mid-turn.
vector_explanations.actions.points.structured_action_types: The ABC turn provides distinct slots — an Action phase for Action cards, a Buy phase for Treasures and purchases, then Cleanup — rather than interchangeable action points.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi: Each game uses only 10 of the base set's 25 kingdom card piles, chosen randomly or from suggested lists, so the buyable card pool changes every play.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: No points are tracked during play; when the game ends every player counts the victory point cards found throughout their entire deck.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: The game ends immediately when the Province pile empties or any three supply piles are exhausted, letting players force the ending by draining piles.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.negative_point_penalti: Curse cards are worth minus 1 VP and are handed out by attacks like the Witch, junking opposing decks with negative points and dead draws.
vector_explanations.turns.variance.out_of_turn_reactions: Reaction cards break the active-player monopoly: revealing Moat from hand makes you immune to an attack card played on another player's turn.

title: 7 Wonders
resolution_core: simultaneous pick-and-pass card drafting over three Ages; point-salad tally with neighbor military

vector_explanations.cards.drafting.pick_and_pass_draft: Each Age deals seven cards; every turn all players keep one card and pass the rest, so hands shrink from seven down to two before the last card is discarded.
vector_explanations.turns.simultaneity.simultaneous_reveal: All players secretly choose their drafted card, then everyone reveals and resolves builds at the same time, keeping even a seven-player game near 30 minutes.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure: The game always lasts exactly three Ages of six picks each, so the military scoring windows at each Age's end are known from the very start.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.card_passing: Passing direction alternates by Age — clockwise in Age I, counterclockwise in Age II, clockwise again in Age III — so you feed different neighbors over time.
vector_explanations.cards.play_patterns.card_tucking: Any drafted card may be tucked face down under your Wonder board to build a Wonder stage, hiding its face while paying the stage's printed resource cost.
vector_explanations.cards.play_patterns.tableau_placement_ca: Played cards accumulate in a color-coded personal tableau: brown resources, grey goods, blue civilian, red military, green science, yellow commerce, purple guilds.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.collection_discounts: Chain symbols grant permanent free construction: a built structure such as the Theater lets you play its linked Age II and III buildings for nothing.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.leftover_resource_conv: At final scoring every full three coins in your treasury convert into one victory point, so leftover cash is never entirely wasted.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.exchange_rates: You may buy a neighbor's resource for two coins per unit; Trading Posts and the Marketplace lower the rate to one coin for the matching neighbor or goods type.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.triggered_scoring_rounds: Military conflicts resolve at the end of each Age: you compare shield totals with both neighbors separately, taking victory or defeat tokens from each comparison.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.escalating_round_multipli: Military victory tokens escalate by Age — worth 1 in Age I, 3 in Age II and 5 in Age III — while every defeat is a flat minus 1 token.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.victory_points_salad: Final score sums seven categories: military tokens, coins, Wonder stages, blue civilian buildings, green science sets, yellow commerce effects and purple guilds.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.set_collection: Green science cards are collected by symbol (compass, gear, tablet), and purple guild cards score for sets of card types built in neighboring cities.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.set_scoring_multiplier: Science scores superlinearly: n identical symbols score n squared points, and each complete set of three different symbols adds seven more.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Aside from Age-end military tokens, everything — civilian, science, guilds, commerce, coins and Wonders — is tallied in one final count after Age III.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.tiebreakers: The rulebook resolves tied victory point totals in favor of the player with the most coins remaining in their treasury.
vector_explanations.economy.production.wood_clay_stone: Brown cards produce wood, stone, clay and ore (grey cards add glass, cloth, papyrus) that are spent in recipe-like combinations to pay building costs.

title: 7 Wonders Duel
resolution_core: alternating picks from a card pyramid; three victory paths (civilian points, military, science)

vector_explanations.economy.card.drafting_pyramid: Each Age arranges 20 cards in an interlocking pyramid of alternating face-up and face-down rows; a card can only be taken once nothing overlaps it.
vector_explanations.cards.drafting.constrained_selection_dra: Only fully uncovered pyramid cards are selectable, so each pick opens new choices (flipping hidden cards face up) and shapes what the opponent may take.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.collection_discounts: Chain symbols on built cards let you construct the matching later building entirely for free, bypassing its whole resource and coin cost.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.alternate_victory_paths: You can win three distinct ways: civilian victory on points after Age III, military supremacy on the Conflict track, or scientific supremacy via six different symbols.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.sudden_death_win_conditi: Pushing the Conflict pawn into the enemy capital or collecting six different science symbols ends the game instantly, regardless of the point totals.
vector_explanations.economy.market.dynamic_prices: Buying a resource from the bank costs 2 coins plus 1 per matching brown or grey production card the opponent owns, so their engine inflates your prices.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.coin: Coins pay for cards and bank resources; discarding a card yields 2 coins plus 1 per yellow building you own, and military looting destroys the enemy's coins.
vector_explanations.cards.drafting.snake_draft: Wonder selection uses a reversing 1-2-1 draft: from four revealed Wonders, the first player takes one, the second takes two, and the first takes the last.
vector_explanations.turns.variance.extra_turns: Several Wonders (such as Piraeus and the Sphinx) print 'immediately play again', granting an extra turn outside the normal strict alternation.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: If neither supremacy fires, players tally civilian buildings, guilds, Wonders, progress tokens, military zone points and coins at three per point after Age III.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure: The game runs exactly three Ages of 20 pyramid cards each, with a different published pyramid shape for Ages I, II and III.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.catch_up_mechanisms: At the start of Ages II and III, the player who is behind on the military track decides which player takes the first turn of the new Age.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi: Setup secretly removes three cards from each Age deck, mixes three random guilds of seven into Age III, and exposes only five of the ten progress tokens.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.set_collection: Science is collected by symbol: a pair of identical symbols immediately grants a progress token, and assembling six different symbols wins outright.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.victory_points_salad: Civilian victory counts points from blue buildings, guilds, Wonder stages, progress tokens, the Conflict pawn's zone and banked coins.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.leftover_resource_conv: Unspent treasury converts at game end: every full three coins is worth one victory point toward the civilian total.

title: Race for the Galaxy
resolution_core: simultaneous role selection; only chosen phases occur; cards discarded from hand as currency

vector_explanations.actions.selection.role_selection: Each round every player picks one of seven identical action cards (Explore, Develop, Settle, Consume, Produce variants); each pick adds that phase for everyone, with a privilege bonus reserved for its chooser.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.simultaneous_selection: Role cards are chosen face down by all players at once, so you must predict which phases opponents will call and leech off their selections.
vector_explanations.turns.simultaneity.simultaneous_reveal: All chosen action cards flip simultaneously, and players then work through the union of selected phases largely in parallel.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.role_selected_phases: Only the phases somebody selected occur that round — if nobody picks Produce, no worlds produce — which makes phase prediction the core skill.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Whatever was picked, the selected phases always resolve in the fixed printed order: Explore, Develop, Settle, Consume, then Produce.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.card_as_currency: There is no money: development and world costs are paid by discarding that many other cards from your hand, making every card potential cash.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_limits: At the end of each round you must discard down to ten cards, punishing hoarding after an oversized Explore turn.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.multi_use_cards: Every card can become a built world or development, be spent as payment, or sit face down on a world as a trade good — and each use forgoes the others.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hidden_hand_information: Hands are private, and goods sit as face-down cards whose identities nobody checks, keeping rivals' payment capacity and plans hidden.
vector_explanations.cards.play_patterns.tableau_placement_ca: Built cards form a personal tableau whose printed powers apply during matching phases; reaching a 12-card tableau triggers the endgame.
vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.tableau_engine: Tableau powers compound: Explore powers draw extra cards, Settle powers discount or conquer worlds, and production worlds refill goods each Produce phase.
vector_explanations.economy.card.tableau_card_combos: Later cards amplify earlier ones — Alien-keyword worlds feed Alien Tech Institute-style bonuses — so tableau sequencing builds combo chains.
vector_explanations.economy.production.resource_generation: Produce phases place a good (a face-down card) on each production world of matching color, while windfall worlds refill only through special powers.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.immediate_incremental_sco: Consume powers cash goods for victory point chips on the spot, doubled by the Consume x2 privilege, steadily draining the shared VP pool.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: The game ends after the round in which the VP chip pool (12 chips per player) empties or any player's tableau reaches twelve or more cards.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.victory_points_salad: Final score sums printed tableau card values, collected VP chips, and the variable bonuses of six-cost developments like Galactic Federation.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Beyond chips claimed during play, all tableau card values and six-cost development bonuses are evaluated only in the final tally.

title: Wingspan
resolution_core: action-cube row activation; habitat rows re-trigger bird powers right-to-left

vector_explanations.actions.points.variable_action_counts: Turn counts shrink each round — 8, then 7, 6 and 5 action cubes — because one cube is retired to the round-goal board at the end of every round.
vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.tableau_engine: Birds played into a habitat row form an engine: taking that row's action re-triggers every bird's brown power, compounding as the row fills toward five birds.
vector_explanations.economy.card.engine_activation: Choosing gain-food, lay-eggs or draw-cards activates the matching habitat row, sweeping its accumulated brown powers from right to left.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.custom_dice_faces: The five birdfeeder dice carry food symbols — invertebrate, seed, fish, fruit, rodent, plus a split seed/invertebrate face — instead of numeric pips.
vector_explanations.economy.market.dice_drafting: The gain-food action drafts dice out of the birdfeeder dice tower; the feeder is refilled only when empty or when all remaining dice show the same face.
vector_explanations.cards.play_patterns.card_tucking: Flocking and predator birds tuck cards face down beneath themselves; each tucked card is worth one point and represents growing flock size.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.hidden_objectives: Each player keeps a secret bonus card (dealt two, keep one at setup) scoring categories like birds with eggs in bowl nests at game end.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.triggered_scoring_rounds: At the end of each of the four rounds the pre-revealed goal tile for that round is evaluated and players' cubes are placed on the goal board.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.majority_rankings: On the green goal-board side, round goals award descending points to first, second and third place in categories such as eggs in grassland birds.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure: The game is exactly four rounds with all goal tiles revealed at setup, so everyone can plan toward all four scoring targets from turn one.
vector_explanations.economy.production.egg_nesting: The lay-eggs action places egg miniatures directly on bird cards, and each bird stores eggs only up to its printed nest capacity.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.escalating_placement_c: Playing a bird into columns two or three of a habitat costs one extra egg, and columns four or five cost two eggs on top of the bird's food cost.
vector_explanations.turns.variance.out_of_turn_reactions: Pink 'once between turns' powers trigger during opponents' turns, such as gaining food whenever another player takes the gain-food action.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi: Random round-goal tiles, randomly dealt bonus cards and a shuffled 170-bird deck change both targets and card availability every game.
vector_explanations.cards.drafting.open_display_draft: The draw-cards action takes from a face-up tray of three birds or blind from the deck, with the tray refilling at the end of your turn.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Final scoring adds bird point values, bonus cards and round goals to one point per egg, per cached food token and per tucked card.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.victory_points_salad: Points arrive from six sources — bird values, bonus cards, round goals, eggs, cached food and tucked cards — so nearly every action scores something.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.solo_mode_design: The base box includes the Automa deck (designed by David Studley), a full solo opponent that takes cards and competes for round goals.

title: Everdell
resolution_core: worker placement plus card tableau city-building across four personally paced seasons

vector_explanations.actions.placement.worker_placement: Workers are deployed to basic locations, forest locations, events and destination cards to gather resources and cards, one placement (or card play) per turn.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.worker_pool_growth: You begin winter with two workers and gain more when preparing for each season — one in spring, one in summer and two in autumn — finishing with six.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.worker_retrieval_timin: 'Prepare for Season' is itself a turn that recalls all your deployed workers; each player chooses when to take it, so the table cycles asynchronously.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.placement_on_opponent_: Open destination cards such as the Inn and Post Office in a rival's city accept your worker, and the card's owner is paid a point token for hosting the visit.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.blocked_spaces: Exclusive locations and destinations hold a single worker while locations marked open accept any number, making placement denial an explicit rules distinction.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.seasonal_cycle_structure: Each player individually advances through winter, spring, summer and autumn; spring and autumn re-trigger green production and summer grants Meadow cards.
vector_explanations.economy.market.worker_placement_seasons: Season state changes the action landscape: Journey destinations only accept workers in autumn, and your chosen preparation timing sets when production re-fires.
vector_explanations.cards.play_patterns.tableau_placement_ca: Critter and construction cards build a personal city capped at fifteen cards, whose printed powers and point values define your entire engine.
vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.tableau_engine: Green production cards like the Farm produce repeatedly, blue governance cards discount or reward plays, and purple prosperity cards convert the city into endgame points.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.collection_discounts: Every construction 'opens' its paired critter — build the Courthouse and the Judge may later be played for free by occupying that construction.
vector_explanations.economy.production.resource_generation: Forest locations and production cards yield the four resources — twigs, resin, pebbles and berries — that pay the costs printed on cards.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.public_milestones: Four basic events and four randomly dealt special events sit face up and are claimed first-come-first-served with a worker for their point value.
vector_explanations.actions.timing.pass_and_out: After autumn a player passes out of the game permanently while opponents keep playing, so the season pacing you chose determines your total number of turns.
vector_explanations.cards.drafting.open_display_draft: The shared Meadow always displays eight face-up cards that may be played or drawn from, refilling from the deck the moment a card leaves.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_limits: Your hand may never exceed eight cards; card gains beyond the limit are forfeited, making the timing of card intake a real decision.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Scores are tallied only after every player has passed: card base values, point tokens, prosperity bonuses and claimed events all count at once.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.victory_points_salad: Points come from card printed values, point tokens earned during play, purple prosperity card bonuses, and both basic and special events.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi: Four random special events, shuffled forest location tiles and a shuffled 128-card deck vary the objectives and market from game to game.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.solo_mode_design: The base rulebook includes official solo rules against Rugwort, an automa villain who occupies locations and claims cards across escalating games.

title: Splendor
resolution_core: take gem chips or buy/reserve cards; card bonuses permanently discount future buys; race to 15 prestige

vector_explanations.economy.production.gem_chips: Poker-weight chips in five gem colors plus gold jokers are the entire economy; a turn can take three different chips, or two matching only if that pile still holds at least four.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.collection_discounts: Each purchased development card is a permanent bonus gem of its color, discounting every future purchase and eventually enabling completely free buys.
vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.engine_to_points: Cheap tier-1 cards carry bonuses but almost no points, so play pivots from hoovering up discounts to buying tier-3 cards worth three to five prestige.
vector_explanations.cards.drafting.open_display_draft: Twelve development cards lie face up in three tier rows of four, and every purchase or reservation is refilled immediately from that tier's deck.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hidden_hand_information: Reserving blindly from the top of a deck puts the card into your hand unseen by rivals — the game's only hidden information besides deck order.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.storage_limits: A hard ten-chip limit applies at the end of your turn; exceeding it forces you to return chips of your choice to the bank immediately.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.finite_supply: Chip piles are deliberately scarce (seven per color at four players, fewer at lower counts), and taking two matching chips is illegal from a pile below four.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: Reaching 15 prestige triggers the end, but the current round is completed so every player has an equal number of turns before totals are compared.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.immediate_incremental_sco: Prestige is banked the instant a pointed card or noble tile arrives, so the standings are always public and exact throughout the game.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.public_milestones: Noble tiles (player count plus one) are openly displayed and visit automatically, first come first served, when your card bonuses meet their requirement, each worth three prestige.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi: Which noble tiles appear and the shuffled order of the three tier decks vary the affordable paths to 15 prestige in every session.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.tiebreakers: Ties at game end are broken in favor of the player who purchased the fewest development cards, rewarding efficiency over volume.
vector_explanations.turns.order.fixed_clockwise: Play always proceeds clockwise from the start player, each player performing exactly one action — take chips, reserve, or buy — with no interrupts.

title: Azul
resolution_core: take-all-of-one-color factory drafting; pattern lines feed a 5x5 mosaic wall with adjacency scoring

vector_explanations.economy.card.tile_drafting_factory: Five to nine factory displays each hold four tiles drawn from the bag; drafting from a factory pushes its leftover tiles into the shared center pool.
vector_explanations.cards.drafting.take_all_of_type_draft: You must take every tile of one color from a single factory or from the center — never a subset — so piles of three or four matching tiles swing turns.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.pattern_building: Drafted tiles load one of five pattern lines sized one to five; a line accepts a single color, and only a completed line tiles the mosaic wall.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.personal_map_building: Each player fills a private 5x5 wall board scored independently; the standard side prints fixed color positions, and a color may appear once per row.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.adjacency_bonuses: A tile placed on the wall scores its full contiguous horizontal and vertical lines, so tiles at busy crossings can pay double-digit points.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.immediate_incremental_sco: During wall-tiling each moved tile is scored on the spot for its row and column adjacency before the next tile is even placed.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.per_round_scoring: Every round ends with the same wall-tiling evaluation: each complete pattern line sends one tile to the wall, scores it, and discards the excess.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.negative_point_penalti: Overflow tiles fall to the floor line, costing -1, -1, -2, -2, -2, -3, -3 in order, and the first-player marker also occupies a floor space.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.row_column_completion_: End bonuses pay 2 points per complete wall row, 7 per complete column, and 10 per color placed all five times.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: The game ends after the round in which any player completes a horizontal wall row of five tiles.
vector_explanations.actions.timing.turn_order_selection: Being first to take tiles from the center also takes the first-player marker: it costs a floor-line penalty point but guarantees opening the next round.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Rounds always run Factory Offer (alternating drafts until every tile is claimed), Wall-tiling, then Preparing the Next Round by refilling factories from the bag.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.tiebreakers: Tied final scores are broken in favor of the player with more complete horizontal lines; if still tied, the rules declare a shared victory.

title: Patchwork
resolution_core: buy polyomino patches with buttons and time; rearmost pawn on the shared time track acts

vector_explanations.spatial.placement.polyomino_fitting: All 33 patches are irregular polyominoes that must be squeezed onto a 9x9 quilt board, and every square left uncovered costs two points at the end.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.personal_map_building: Each player sews onto a private quilt board; opponents can never touch your grid, only outrace you to patches, leather scraps and buttons.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.tile_rotation_orientat: Patches may be freely rotated and mirrored before sewing, and finding the orientation that avoids dead squares is the core spatial puzzle.
vector_explanations.actions.timing.time_track_turns: Both pawns share one time track and whoever is furthest behind takes the next turn, so buying a cheap patch can grant several consecutive turns.
vector_explanations.actions.timing.time_as_resource: Every patch costs buttons and time spaces, and the alternative skip action converts time directly into buttons at one per space crossed.
vector_explanations.turns.order.turn_order_by_track_position: The rearmost time-track pawn always acts next (with the top pawn moving first from a shared space), so turn order emerges purely from spending decisions.
vector_explanations.economy.production.button_economy: Button icons stitched onto placed patches pay out every time your pawn crosses one of the nine button-income spaces on the time track.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.coin: Buttons are simultaneously the game's coin and its victory points: spent on patches during play and counted raw in the final score.
vector_explanations.cards.drafting.constrained_selection_dra: Only the three patches clockwise ahead of the neutral spool token may be bought, and each purchase moves the spool, sliding the selection window.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.negative_point_penalti: Final scoring subtracts two points for every empty square remaining on your 9x9 quilt board, so incomplete quilts bleed points.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.public_milestones: The 7x7 special tile is a one-time public bonus worth seven points, claimed by the first player to complete a fully covered 7x7 area.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Once both pawns reach the track's final space, scores settle as buttons held, plus the 7x7 bonus, minus two per empty quilt square.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: The center space of the time track is the finish line; the game ends only after both players' time tokens have arrived there.

title: Codenames
resolution_core: one-word-plus-number clues; teams guess words on a 5x5 grid against a hidden key card

vector_explanations.cooperation.communication.dedicated_clue: Each team appoints a spymaster who alone consults the key card and may communicate with the field operatives only through the clue channel.
vector_explanations.cooperation.communication.coded_communic: Clues are locked to a fixed grammar — exactly one word plus one number — and the word may not be any visible codename on the table or a direct form of one.
vector_explanations.cooperation.communication.limited_table_: Operatives may debate freely, but spymasters must keep a straight face and add nothing beyond the clue; illegal extra hints forfeit per the rules.
vector_explanations.deduction.inference.associative_clue_int: Operatives interpret loose semantic associations rather than logic: 'nut: 2' might bind ACORN and BARK while steering clear of the assassin word.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.asymmetric_knowled: Both spymasters see the full key card mapping all 25 words; operatives see only the accumulating cover cards, and the game lives in that gap.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.team_vs_team: Red and blue teams cooperate internally and race externally, alternating clue-and-guess turns until one side's agents are all contacted.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.sudden_death_win_conditi: Touching the assassin word ends the game instantly in a loss for the guessing team, no matter how many agents either side has found.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.race_to_finish: There is no point score: the first team to contact all of its agents — or to benefit from the rivals touching the assassin — simply wins.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.triggered_forced_r: Each word an operative touches compels the spymaster to disclose its true identity at once by covering it with an agent, bystander or assassin card.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.seat_order_compensation: The key card gives the starting team nine agents to find against the other team's eight, compensating the second team for moving later.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi: Setup deals 25 random word cards from a double-sided deck of 200 plus one of 40 key cards in a random orientation, so boards never repeat.
vector_explanations.meta.real_time.sand_timers: The box includes a sand timer the rules offer for pressuring any player who deliberates too long — either side may flip it on the other.
vector_explanations.luck.push.escalating_risk: A team must guess at least once and may continue up to the clue number plus one; every extra guess risks a bystander, an enemy agent, or the assassin.

title: The Crew: The Quest for Planet Nine
resolution_core: cooperative must-follow trick-taking; assigned task cards must be won by the right player

vector_explanations.cards.play_patterns.trick_taking: The deck is 36 suit cards (four colors numbered 1-9) plus four rockets; each trick's winner collects the cards and leads the next trick.
vector_explanations.cards.play_patterns.must_follow_suit: Players must follow the led color whenever able; only a void permits playing rockets or discarding off-color, so hand distribution is a constraint system.
vector_explanations.cards.play_patterns.trump_suits: The four rocket cards (values 1-4) form a permanent trump suit, and the holder of the rocket 4 is the commander who leads the first trick.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.fully_cooperative: The whole table wins or loses each mission together; there are no individual scores anywhere in the 50-mission campaign.
vector_explanations.cooperation.communication.limited_table_: Talking about the cards in your hand is expressly forbidden — no hints about holdings or wishes — outside the sanctioned radio communication action.
vector_explanations.cooperation.communication.sanctioned_non: Once per mission each player may lay one card face up and place the radio token at its top, middle or bottom, signaling it is their highest, only, or lowest card of that color.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.cooperative_win_loss: A mission succeeds only if every task is completed, and it fails the instant any task becomes impossible, forcing an immediate collective replay.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.scenario_objective_victo: Each logbook mission defines its own victory terms — which tasks must be won by whom, in what order, or special conditions like a player winning no tricks.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.campaign_progression_out: Progress is recorded mission by mission in the logbook, with the number of attempts per mission serving as the campaign's running performance measure.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.staged_escalation_: The logbook's 50 missions escalate steadily, layering more task cards and stricter ordering token constraints as the crew nears Planet Nine.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.contract_fulfillment: Task cards are contracts: the exact suited card shown must be captured in a trick won specifically by the crew member who claimed that task.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hidden_hand_information: Hands stay hidden even between teammates, so the cooperative puzzle must be solved through inference from plays and the single radio signal.
vector_explanations.cooperation.assistance.restricted_teamma: The official distress signal rule lets each player pass exactly one card (never a rocket) to a neighbor before a hard mission, at a penalty noted in the log.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi: The full deck is dealt out every mission and task cards are drawn randomly from their own deck, so the same mission number plays differently each attempt.

title: Hanabi
resolution_core: inverted hands; spend clock tokens on color/number clues to build five fireworks piles 1-5

vector_explanations.cards.hand.inverted_hand_visibility: Cards are held facing away from their owner, so you see every hand at the table except your own and must act on remembered clues about your cards.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.fully_cooperative: All players build the five fireworks together and share one final score; there is no individual reward, hidden agenda, or single winner.
vector_explanations.cooperation.communication.coded_communic: Information flows only through the formal hint action — name one color or one number and point out every card in that hand matching it, nothing more.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.paid_information_a: Every hint spends one of the team's eight clock tokens; tokens come back only by discarding a card or by completing a firework with its 5.
vector_explanations.deduction.inference.negative_inference: Because a hint must indicate all matching cards in a hand, the cards conspicuously not pointed at are known to lack that color or number.
vector_explanations.cooperation.assistance.shared_resources: The eight clock tokens and three fuse tokens are collective pools, so one player's wasted hint starves everyone else's future turns.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.graded_victory_levels: The rulebook grades the final total on a 0-25 ladder of festival reviews, up to the legendary 25 for completing all five fireworks.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.cooperative_win_loss: Misplaying a card flips a fuse token, and the third fuse ends the game in immediate collective defeat for the whole team.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_replenishment: After playing or discarding you immediately draw back up to your hand size — five cards with two or three players, four cards with four or five.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.discard_costs: Discarding is a deliberate action that fuels the economy: it regains a clock token but risks burning the only remaining copy of a needed card.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: When the last card is drawn, every player including the drawer takes exactly one final turn before the fireworks are scored.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.set_collection: The team collectively builds five color-ordered piles from 1 up to 5, and the final score is the sum of the top card of each pile.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.transparent_distributions: The deck's exact composition is printed in the rules — three 1s, two each of 2 through 4, and a single 5 per color — so every discard risk is calculable.

title: Love Letter
resolution_core: draw one, play one from a 16-card deck; last player standing or highest card wins the round

vector_explanations.scoring.victory.elimination_victory: A round is won on the spot if every rival is knocked out — by a correct Guard guess, a lost Baron comparison, or being forced to discard the Princess.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.transparent_distributions: The 16-card distribution — five Guards, two each of Priest, Baron, Handmaid and Prince, one King, Countess and Princess — is printed on the reference cards.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.card_counting_memory: All played and discarded cards lie face up in front of players by rule, so counting down the shrinking deck powers accurate Guard accusations.
vector_explanations.deduction.inference.negative_inference: A missed Guard guess proves the target does not hold that character, and a survived Baron comparison bounds what the survivor can be holding.
vector_explanations.deduction.inference.question_asking: The Guard is a structured query: name any character except Guard, and the target must truthfully confirm a hit by revealing and leaving the round.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.triggered_forced_r: Card effects compel disclosure — a correct Guard call reveals and eliminates, the Baron forces a private hand comparison, and the Prince forces a face-up discard.
vector_explanations.deduction.information.asymmetric_knowled: The Priest grants a private peek at one hand and the Baron shows hands only to the two players involved, creating pockets of privileged knowledge.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: Your entire hand is one card; each turn you draw a second and must choose which of the two to play and which to keep as your hidden identity.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.per_round_scoring: Each round's winner earns one token of affection, and rounds repeat with a fresh 16-card shuffle until someone reaches the required total.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.race_to_finish: The game ends the moment a player claims their final token — seven tokens at two players, five at three, four at four.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: If the deck empties, the round halts immediately and the remaining players compare hands, the highest-value character claiming the token.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.tiebreakers: Deck-out ties are broken by summing the values of each tied player's discarded cards, with the highest discard total winning the round.

title: Jaipur
resolution_core: take goods or camels from a five-card market, or sell sets on descending price-token ladders

vector_explanations.cards.drafting.open_display_draft: The market is always five face-up cards between the two players, refilled from the draw pile the moment any cards leave it.
vector_explanations.cards.drafting.take_all_of_type_draft: Taking camels means taking every camel currently in the market at once, which refills the row with unseen cards for your opponent's next turn.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.sell_action: Selling is a dedicated action: discard any number of cards of one good for its tokens, with diamonds, gold and silver requiring at least two cards per sale.
vector_explanations.economy.market.resource_pricing: Each good has its own descending token ladder — diamond, gold and silver ladders start at 7 or 6 while leather runs down from 4 — pricing scarcity into every sale.
vector_explanations.economy.barter.commodity_money: Camels function as living currency: they never occupy your hand but are traded card-for-card into the market during multi-good exchanges.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_limits: Your hand may never exceed seven cards and you cannot take goods that would break the cap, while camels sit outside the hand in an exempt herd.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: Under the seven-card cap you constantly weigh holding goods toward a bigger bonus sale against selling early to grab the top-value tokens.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.set_liquidation: Goods are hoarded and then liquidated in batches, and the camel-exchange action lets you assemble large sales before a token ladder collapses.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.nonlinear_set_scoring: Selling three, four, or five-plus cards at once adds a secret bonus token worth 1-3, 4-6 or 8-10 extra points, making one big sale beat several small ones.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.collection_majorities: At round end the camel token awards five points to whichever player holds the larger herd, punishing a trader who ignored camels entirely.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.hidden_score_tracking: Bonus tokens are drawn face down and kept secret, so neither trader knows exactly who leads until the round is fully scored.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.per_round_scoring: Each round ends in a complete scoring and its winner takes a Seal of Excellence; the first player to two seals wins the best-of-three match.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: A round ends immediately when three goods-token stacks are empty or the draw pile runs out, so a leader can deliberately race the clock.

title: Sushi Go!
resolution_core: simultaneous pick-and-pass drafting over three rounds; set-collection scoring

vector_explanations.cards.drafting.pick_and_pass_draft: Every player secretly keeps one card from their hand and passes the rest on, hands shrinking with each pick until the round's cards are exhausted.
vector_explanations.turns.simultaneity.simultaneous_reveal: Picks are placed face down and flipped over simultaneously, so every player commits before seeing what the table kept this pick.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.card_passing: Whole hands circulate to the left after every pick, so you always know exactly which cards you passed and can track them around the table.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.set_collection: Tempura scores only in pairs (5 points) and sashimi only in triples (10 points), so half-finished sets are worth exactly zero.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.nonlinear_set_scoring: Dumplings score on an escalating 1/3/6/10/15 ladder for one through five or more, rewarding deep commitment to a single set.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.collection_majorities: Maki roll icons are compared at each round's end: the most icons scores 6 points and second place 3, with ties splitting points rounded down.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.negative_point_penalti: Pudding cards swing both ways at game end: the most puddings gains 6 points and the fewest loses 6 (the penalty is skipped in two-player games).
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.per_round_scoring: Each of the three rounds is scored separately on the pad as soon as its cards run out — everything except puddings resets between rounds.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Pudding cards are the only holdover, evaluated once after round three for the +6 and -6 majority swing that often decides the winner.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure: The game is always exactly three drafting rounds, with hand size set by player count — ten cards at two players down to seven at five.
vector_explanations.turns.variance.extra_turns: Chopsticks wait in your tableau until you call 'Sushi Go!' on a later pick to take two cards at once, returning the chopsticks to the passing hand.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.tiebreakers: Tied final scores are broken in favor of the player holding the most pudding cards, giving desserts value even outside the majority race.
